what_it_does: States a single architectural law that keeps the Obsidian host a display lens rather than the canonical master: canonical edges live only in the frontmatter links array, while wikilinks, Dataview results, and graph views are derived, regenerable projections that must never be parsed back.
observations: Frontmatter carries id, board:// address, type=block, source=claude_code, state=current, and a part_of link to item-80c37615.
The title frames the content as P3 · 1 · The ONE law that makes 'host not master' mechanical.
The core rule is that edges have exactly one source of truth: the links: [{kind,target}] array resolved by one resolver.
Wikilinks, Dataview query results, and graph nodes are explicitly labeled as derived projections, never canonical edges.

The boundary is defined as a read-back prohibition: the runtime may emit wikilinks and links companions into the vault, but nothing may interpret an Obsidian wikilink, file move, or rename as a change to the canonical edge set.
Canonical filenames must stay as <id>.md; human-readable titles are display concerns only.
The runtime's YAML serializer is the only canonical frontmatter writer, using yaml.dump(sort_keys=False).
A warning notes that Obsidian's serializer can reorder keys and mangle nested links/history arrays.
standouts: The 'host not master' principle is reduced to a mechanical read-back prohibition.
Canonical identity is the links array, not file paths or wikilinks.
Canonical filenames and YAML serialization are corollary laws derived from the same rule.
conventions: Follows the noticeboard item schema with YAML frontmatter, board:// address, id.md filename, links and history arrays.
Uses a declarative, numbered design-law style with corollaries.
concerns: The 'one resolver' and the mechanism that enforces the read-back prohibition are asserted but not defined in this file.
The claim that Obsidian's serializer mangles nested arrays is stated without examples or version context.
The strong prohibition may complicate legitimate user edits made directly inside Obsidian.
notes: This file encodes the central 'host not master' boundary as a concrete read-back prohibition on Obsidian-derived links, moves, and renames.
questions: What does P3 · 1 refer to in this context?
What is the 'one resolver' and where is it implemented?
How is the read-back prohibition enforced technically?
What happens if Obsidian's serializer does write the canonical frontmatter?
How are display titles and aliases managed if filenames must stay as <id>.md?
purpose_vs_actual: yes — the file states and elaborates the single law that makes 'host not master' mechanical, exactly as its title claims.
intention_signals: It tries to prevent Obsidian from silently becoming the schema master by forbidding reverse interpretation of its UI artifacts, and to make that boundary mechanically checkable through filename and YAML-serialization rules.
novelty: It reframes 'host not master' not as a vague principle but as a precise read-back prohibition, treating wikilinks and graph artifacts as one-way emitted projections rather than authoritative relationships.
contribution: Contributes a crisp invariant and boundary rule for host/runtime separation, plus concrete corollaries on filename and YAML serialization that a fused system would keep as guardrails for edge integrity.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard design-law item declaring that canonical edges live only in the frontmatter links array, that Obsidian wikilinks and file moves are one-way derived projections, and that reading them back is prohibited to keep the host from becoming the master.
intention_for_embedding: To make the 'host not master' principle mechanically enforceable by defining a single source of truth for edges and forbidding reverse interpretation of host-generated links and renames.
